ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists for grilled cheese abalone with aburi salmon specifically. FOODON_03311737 (seafood dish) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC (Fish and seafood products) is the closest defensible parent. No specific code exists for this composite abalone-salmon-cheese preparation.

serving.description: 1 plate (approximately 2 pieces abalone + 2 pieces aburi salmon)
serving.natural_language_portion: One serving is approximately 180 g, comprising two pieces of grilled cheese abalone and two pieces of aburi (flame-seared) salmon, as typically served at a Japanese sushi restaurant in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Estimated from USDA FoodData Central: Abalone, mixed species, raw (FDC ID 174183) and Salmon, Atlantic, farmed, raw (FDC ID 175167), combined with standard cheese topping and sushi rice component estimates from USDA SR Legacy
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry exists for this composite dish in HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, or FAO/INFOODS SEA. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted component averaging: approximately 30% abalone (raw, USDA FDC 174183), 30% Atlantic salmon farmed raw (USDA FDC 175167), 15% cheddar cheese (USDA FDC 173414), 20% sushi rice cooked (USDA SR Legacy), and 5% mayonnaise (USDA FDC 172741). Cooking losses and cheese grilling effects are accounted for by standard USDA cooking retention factors. Values represent a best-estimate composite per 100 g of the finished dish.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of sushi rice and mixed seafood dish — analogous estimate based on Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for this composite dish. GI is estimated at 54 (low) based on the dominant carbohydrate component being sushi rice (GI ~55 per University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care 31:2281-2283). The protein and fat content from abalone, salmon, and cheese substantially blunt the glycemic response relative to plain rice. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (54 × 18.4) / 100 ≈ 9.9, where carbohydrate per 180 g serving = 10.2 × 1.8 = 18.36 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content approximately 310 mg per 100 g; a full serving (~180 g) delivers approximately 558 mg sodium, representing about 24% of the 2,300 mg daily limit recommended by the American Heart Association.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to one serving and avoid additional high-sodium condiments such as soy sauce or ponzu when consuming this dish.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low-to-medium glycemic index (~54) driven by sushi rice component; glycemic load per serving is approximately 9.9, which is moderate and manageable within a controlled carbohydrate meal plan.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes can include this dish in moderation, pairing it with non-starchy vegetables and monitoring total carbohydrate intake, as the protein and fat from salmon, abalone, and cheese help attenuate the postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Salmon provides beneficial omega-3 fatty acids (EPA and DHA) which are cardioprotective, but the cheese topping contributes saturated fat (~2.9 g per 100 g) and the dish carries moderate sodium.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The omega-3 content from Atlantic salmon is beneficial for cardiovascular health; however, individuals with cardiovascular disease should be mindful of the saturated fat from cheese and the sodium load, and should limit this dish to one serving per meal.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Grilled Cheese Abalone and Aburi Salmon is a Japanese-style composite seafood dish served at sushi restaurants in Singapore, notably at Sushi Express. The dish combines two distinct preparations: grilled abalone topped with melted cheese, and aburi (flame-seared) salmon, which is briefly torched with a culinary blowtorch to create a caramelised, slightly smoky exterior while retaining a tender, semi-raw interior. The abalone is typically small to medium in size, grilled until tender, and finished with a layer of melted cheese — often a mild cheddar or processed cheese blend — giving it a rich, savoury, umami-forward flavour. The aburi salmon pieces are commonly served atop sushi rice and may be finished with a drizzle of mayonnaise or a light sauce before torching. The overall flavour profile is rich, savoury, and slightly smoky, with the oceanic sweetness of abalone and salmon balanced by the creaminess of melted cheese. Texturally, the dish offers a contrast between the firm, chewy abalone and the silky, lightly seared salmon. Nutritionally, the dish is a moderate-calorie, high-protein option (~13.5 g protein per 100 g) with beneficial omega-3 fatty acids from salmon, calcium from cheese, and micronutrients including vitamin B12, vitamin D, selenium, and phosphorus. The sushi rice component contributes moderate carbohydrates with a low glycemic index. The dish is not suitable for individuals with shellfish, fish, or dairy allergies. Clinically, the sodium content warrants attention for individuals managing hypertension, and the moderate glycemic load requires portion awareness for those with Type 2 Diabetes.

confidence_reason: No direct entry for this composite dish exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, or FAO/INFOODS SEA. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted component averaging of USDA FoodData Central entries for abalone, Atlantic salmon, cheddar cheese, sushi rice, and mayonnaise. GI is an analogous estimate based on the sushi rice component from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are component-weighted estimates; no direct laboratory analysis or approved-source entry exists for this exact dish. Values should be treated as indicative and updated if a direct HPB or USDA entry becomes available. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are all estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data and are not individually reported by a single approved source for this composite dish. 3. The dish is served at Sushi Express - Funan (GrabFood Singapore listing) at SGD 13.00. 4. Halal status is not confirmed; abalone and salmon are permissible under Islamic dietary law but the cheese source and preparation environment are unverified. 5. GI set to 54 (low) as an analogous estimate based on sushi rice GI from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care). 6. No -1 sentinel values are used for core macros; all secondary nutrient -1 values are not applicable here as all secondary nutrients have been estimated from USDA component data and are reported as positive numerics. All numeric fields are populated with estimated values from USDA FoodData Central; no -1 sentinel values remain in this record.
